g.data-narration: Welcome to Physical Design of IoT - the hardware foundation that enables smart devices to interact with the physical world.
The physical design encompasses tangible hardware - sensors, actuators, processors, and communication modules that collect data and perform actions.
g.text: What is Physical Design of IoT?
The tangible hardware components that form the foundation of IoT systems.
These devices bridge the physical and digital worlds - collecting data, processing it,
and performing actions based on that data.

g.data-narration: Every IoT system has five essential building blocks: sensors for input, actuators for output, processing units, communication modules, and power systems.
Sensors are input devices that detect physical properties like temperature, humidity, motion, and light, converting them into electrical signals.
g.text: Detect and measure physical properties from the environment → Convert to electrical signals

g.data-narration: Actuators are output devices that convert electrical signals into physical actions - motors move, relays switch, LEDs display, and buzzers sound.
g.text: Convert electrical signals into physical actions

g.data-narration: Microcontrollers (MCUs) are compact for specific tasks like Arduino and ESP32. Microprocessors (MPUs) run full operating systems for complex gateways like Raspberry Pi.
g.text: Powerful, run full OS for gateways

g.data-narration: Communication modules connect IoT devices using wireless (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, Cellular) or wired (Ethernet, RS-485) technologies.
Power management is critical for battery-operated IoT devices. Common sources include batteries, solar panels, and energy harvesting with techniques like sleep modes.
IoT devices follow a clear architecture: sensors collect data, microcontrollers process it, actuators perform actions, and communication modules connect to networks.
IoT devices fall into three categories: Constrained (simple sensors with limited power), Intermediate (smart home devices), and Gateway devices (full computers like Raspberry Pi).
When designing IoT hardware, consider environmental factors, reliability (MTBF), physical security, and total cost including deployment and maintenance.
Emerging trends include SoC integration, edge AI/ML capabilities, energy harvesting advances, miniaturization with MEMS, and flexible wearable electronics.
For exams: Always list the five key components, compare wireless protocols using tables, address design constraints, and use real-world examples like smart homes.
g.text: 1. Always list the 5 key components (sensors, actuators, MCU, comm, power)
2. Compare wireless protocols using range/data rate/power consumption table
3. Address design constraints: power, cost, environment, security

g.data-narration: To summarize, IoT physical design includes sensors, actuators, processors, communication modules, and power systems - all working together to bridge physical and digital worlds.
g.text: Physical Design enables IoT to interact with the physical world
